test.name: A record-type premise in the question does not narrow a death plan (probate included)
test.description: wilkins-first-plan: q_001 asks 'Using Kentucky death certificates, what were the date and place of death of Elijah Wilkins (b. c. 1814)?' — but the locality survey shows statewide Kentucky certificates began 1911 while the subject (last documented 1870) almost certainly died decades earlier, and county registers have 1860s-1870s gaps. A sound first plan searches the presupposed certificate/register collections AND the other primary death record types — probate/estate records above all. Guards the SKILL.md 'a record type named in the question is a lead, not a scope limit' improvement (issue #657 diagnosis: two headless runs planned certificates only and each attached a different same-named man's certificate).

input.user_message: Create the first research plan for q_001 — using Kentucky death certificates, when and where did Elijah Wilkins die?

judge_context: The plan MUST include at least one plan item targeting probate/estate records (record_type 'probate' — administrator bonds, estate settlements, probate registers at the Muhlenberg County court), alongside any death-certificate/register items. The collections-search fixture surfaces 'Kentucky Probate Records, 1727-1990', the wiki fixtures state probate is the primary death evidence for pre-1911 Kentucky deaths, and volume_search surfaces the browse-only Muhlenberg probate volumes.

Searching the presupposed certificate/register collections (Kentucky Deaths 1911-1967, Deaths and Burials 1843-1970) is fine and expected — the failure this test guards is a plan limited to them. If NO plan item targets probate/estate records, score Record type selection as 1 (fail).

Ideal (grade generously, do not require verbatim): a rationale somewhere in the plan notes the era/coverage mismatch — statewide certificates begin 1911 while the subject was last documented alive in 1870, so the presupposed record type likely cannot cover the death and alternatives are needed.
Additional breadth items (church burials, cemetery/Find a Grave, censuses/tax lists to bracket the disappearance, an Ancestry fallback) strengthen the plan but are not individually required.
